title: Aggregate Demand and Aggregate Supply
paperName: Cambridge International AS Level Economics (P1 & P2)
summary: 9708 AS macro — AD–AS model, equilibrium, shifts, and policy analysis with GeoGebra interactive diagram.
learningObjectives: Define aggregate demand and its components.
Draw AD, SRAS, and LRAS curves.
Analyse effects of AD and AS shifts on P and Y.
Distinguish short-run and long-run macroeconomic equilibrium.

simpleExplanation.title: The whole economy on one graph
simpleExplanation.summary: AD–AS combines micro supply/demand ideas at economy-wide level. Equilibrium real GDP and price level sit where AD crosses AS.
simpleExplanation.analogy: AD is total shopping demand for everything in the economy; SRAS is how much firms will produce at each price level today; LRAS is the factory's maximum sustainable capacity.
steps: AD slopes down — wealth effect, interest rate effect, exchange rate effect.
SRAS slopes up — input costs rise as economy overheats in short run.
LRAS vertical at Yf — classical view of full employment output.
Shift AD or AS — predict new P and Y; state transmission mechanism.

flashcards.back: AD = C + I + G + (X − M) — consumption, investment, government spending, net exports.
Wealth effect (lower P → higher real wealth → more C), interest rate effect (lower P → lower r → more I and C), exchange rate effect (lower P → currency appreciation → fewer exports).
As the price level rises, firms' output prices increase faster than input costs (sticky wages), so profit margins rise and firms supply more output.
Long-run aggregate supply — vertical at full employment output (Yf), determined by factors of production and technology, independent of price level.

flashcards.front: AD shifts right — effect on P and Y?
flashcards.back: Both price level (P) and real GDP (Y) rise in the short run — demand-pull pressure on prices and higher output.

flashcards.front: SRAS shifts left — effect on P and Y?
flashcards.back: Price level rises but real GDP falls — stagflation (cost-push inflation with lower output).

sections.content: **AD–AS** is the core 9708 macro diagram — used for **fiscal policy**, **monetary policy**, **inflation**, and **unemployment** essays. Practise drawing **three curves** with labelled axes and showing **E₁ → E₂** after every shift.
**Aggregate demand** is total planned spending on domestically produced goods and services at each price level. It slopes **downward** because of the wealth, interest rate, and exchange rate effects.

AD = C + I + G + (X − M)

Equilibrium: AD = AS

Inflationary gap: Y > Yf

Recessionary gap: Y < Yf

items: **SRAS** slopes upward — sticky wages and prices mean firms respond to higher P by increasing output.
**LRAS** is vertical at **Yf** — in the long run, output is determined by supply-side capacity.
**Recessionary gap**: equilibrium Y below Yf — cyclical unemployment.
**Inflationary gap**: equilibrium Y above Yf — demand-pull inflation risk.

sections.question:
An economy is initially in long-run equilibrium at Yf = $800bn and P = 100. Consumer confidence rises and AD increases by $40bn at every price level.

(a) On an AD–AS diagram, describe the shift.
(b) Predict the new equilibrium P and Y in the short run (assume SRAS is upward sloping).
(c) What happens in the long run if AD stays at the higher level?
sections.solution:
(a) **Diagram**
Draw AD₁ and AD₂ with AD₂ **to the right** of AD₁ (parallel shift right by $40bn). Mark initial equilibrium E₁ at AD₁ ∩ SRAS. New short-run equilibrium E₂ at AD₂ ∩ SRAS.

(b) **Short-run effect**
Both **P rises** (e.g. from 100 to ~105) and **Y rises** (e.g. from $800bn to ~$830bn). The economy moves into an **inflationary gap** (Y > Yf).

(c) **Long-run adjustment**
If AD remains high, wages and costs adjust upward. **SRAS shifts left** until Y returns to **Yf** but at a **higher price level** — demand-pull inflation without permanent output gain.

sections.content: Always label **P** (price level) and **Y** (real GDP) on axes. Show **initial equilibrium E₁** and **new equilibrium E₂** after a shift — arrows on curves, not just prose.

steps.caption: AD = C + I + G + (X − M) — downward sloping in P–Y space.
steps.embedHint: Identify AD curve and axes (price level vs real GDP).

steps.caption: SRAS slopes upward — higher P encourages greater output in short run.
LRAS vertical at full employment output Yf.
steps.embedHint: Mark Yf — long-run equilibrium independent of price level.

steps.caption: Shift AD right → higher P and Y (demand-pull inflation risk).
steps.embedHint: Shift AD and read new equilibrium P and Y.
